article:
Chunk # 0.
.4FiALY’MCAL BIOCHEMISTRY i?‘& 299-304 (1968) 
An Improved Technique for the Isolation 
of Mitochondria from Plant Tissue 
N F HAARDI AND H 0 HULTIN 
Department of Food Science and Technology, University of Massachusetts, 
Amherst, Massachusetts 01002 
Received October 10, 1967 
Difficulties are often encountered in the extraction of active mitochon- 
dria from plant tissues that contain high concentrations of organic acids, 
tannins, and other substances capable of inactivating the enzymic systems 
during and after homogenization Attempts have been made to overcome 
some of these difficulties by varying the method of tissue maceration (1)) 
the use of antioxidants (2) or tannin-complexing agents (3), and by 
incorporation of high concentrations of buffer into the extracting media 
(4) Methods of mitochondrial preparation from fruit tissue have often 
resulted in particulate fractions of low and inconsistent respiratory 
activity The technique for isolating mitochondria described here offers a 
method for the consistent preparation of active mitochondria from the 
pulp of the ripening banana and other plant tissue The preparation of 
mitochondria even from green fruit capable of performing several basic 
mitochondrial functions consistently indicates that this procedure may 
be widely adaptable to many difficult-to-handle plant tissues 
PROCEDURE 
Approximately 150 gm of banana pulp was cut into slices about 1 mm 
t.hick and immersed immediately into liquid nitrogen The excess liquid 
nitrogen was drained off, and the frozen slices were pulverized to a 
powder by six 1 set treatments in a Waring Blendor set at high speed 
The powdered tissue, still frozen, was slowly mixed with 400 ml of ex- 
tracting solution containing sucrose, 160 mmoles ; EDTA, 0.2 mmole ; 
and 4 gm casein (Nutritional Biochemicals Corp.) prepared by dis- 
solving in an appropriate amount of KOH solution and partially purify- 
ing by centrifugation and dialysis against 0.01 M phosphate buffer, pH 
7.3 
This mixture of banana tissue was maintained at an apparent pH of 
8.2 with 20% KOH during extraction by means of a Beckman model G 
’ Present address: Institute for Enzyme Research, IJniversity of Wisconsin, Madi- 
son, Wisconsin 53706 
299 300 HAARD AND HULTIN 
pH meter Since the temperature of the reaction mixture was below that 
at which a meaningful pH measurement could be made, the pH of 8.2 
was not a true pH It was found, however, that maintenance of the read- 
ing at 8.2 on this instrument allowed one to obtain a true pH value of 
approximately 7.3 when the temperature of the mixture rose to a level 
at which the instrument could read pH accurately With a different 
instrument, another calibration would have to be made 
The cold mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 r-pm in the GSA 
rotor of a Sorvall RC-2 refrigerated centrifuge The residues were dis- 
carded, and the supernatant fraction was centrifuged for 15 min at 
30,000 rpm in the No 30 rotor of a Spinco model L ultracentrifuge The 
pellets were suspended with a Potter-type homogenizer in 50 ml of a 
solution which contained sucrose, 20 mmoles; Tris [tris (hydroxymethyl) 
aminomethane] buffer, pH 7.5, 0.5 mmole; ATP, 160 ,pmoles; MgCl,, 
250 ,poles; and 250 mg bovine serum albumin (BSA) The suspension 
was centrifuged for 5 min at 4000 rpm in the No 30 rotor of the Spinco 
model L ultracentrifuge; the supernatant fraction was decanted and 
centrifuged at 30,000 rpm for 15 min The residue from the last centrif- 
ugation was homogenized in 5 ml of the latter solution All centrifuga- 
tion procedures were carried out at &4”C The speed of centrifugation 
required to sediment the mitochondria was determined by measuring 
succinic oxidase activities of fractions prepared at several gravitational 
forces The high force necessary to obtain a good yield was due to the 
high viscosity of the mixture probably caused by extraction of materials 
such as the pectic substances 
Oxygen consumption in the presence of Krebs cycle intermediates was 
measured with a Gilson differential respirometer Protein was deter- 
mined by a modified Folin technique (5)) and the results were corrected 
for the BSA introduced with the suspending medium 
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION 
The mitochondria,l suspension was brilliant yellow and contained 
mobile particles about 1 p in diameter which gave a positive Janus 
green B test (6) The appearance of the particles in the electron micro- 
scope was typical of mitochondria (Fig 1) 
The ripeness of the fruit used had no influence on either the amount 
of particulate protein in the preparation, which was generally about 
20-30 mg from 150 gm of pulp, or the respiratory activity of the prep- 
aration with various Krebs cycle intermediates (Table 1)

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

1. Cut approximately 150 gm of banana pulp into slices about 1 mm thick and immediately immerse them in liquid nitrogen.
2. Drain off the excess liquid nitrogen and pulverize the frozen slices to a powder using a Waring Blendor set at high speed.
3. Slowly mix the powdered tissue, still frozen, with 400 ml of extracting solution containing sucrose (160 mmoles), EDTA (0.2 mmole), and 4 gm casein.
4. Maintain the mixture at an apparent pH of 8.2 with 20% KOH during extraction.
5. Centrifuge the cold mixture for 10 min at 5000 rpm in a Sorvall RC-2 refrigerated centrifuge using the GSA rotor. Discard the residues and collect the supernatant fraction.
6. Centrifuge the supernatant fraction for 15 min at 30,000 rpm in a Spinco model L ultracentrifuge using the No 30 rotor. Collect the pellets.
7. Suspend the pellets in 50 ml of a solution containing sucrose (20 mmoles), Tris buffer (pH 7.5, 0.5 mmole), ATP (160 pmoles), MgCl2 (250 pmoles), and bovine serum albumin (250 mg).
8. Centrifuge the suspension for 5 min at 4000 rpm in the No 30 rotor of the Spinco model L ultracentrifuge. Decant the supernatant fraction.
9. Centrifuge the decanted fraction at 30,000 rpm for 15 min. Collect the residue.
10. Homogenize the residue in 5 ml of the latter solution.
11. All centrifugation procedures should be carried out at 4°C.
12. Measure oxygen consumption in the presence of Krebs cycle intermediates using a Gilson differential respirometer.
13. Determine protein concentration using a modified Folin technique and correct the results for the bovine serum albumin introduced with the suspending medium.

**Protocol Completeness and Gaps:**
The protocol provided in the article is relatively complete, describing the steps involved in isolating mitochondria from plant tissue. However, there are a few gaps in the protocol. The article does not provide specific details about the composition or preparation of the extracting solution, such as the concentration of KOH used or the method of dissolving and purifying the casein. Additionally, the article does not mention the exact speed of centrifugation for the first step or the exact speed and duration of centrifugation for the second step. These missing details could affect the reproducibility of the protocol.

No organisms mentioned.

summary:
Revised Summary: An improved technique for isolating mitochondria from plant tissue was developed by N F Haardi and H Hultin from the Department of Food Science and Technology at the University of Massachusetts, Amherst. They overcame difficulties caused by high concentrations of organic acids and other substances by using enzymic systems and tannin-complexing agents. The researchers focused on the pulp of ripening bananas and other plant tissues. They employed liquid nitrogen treatment, pulverization, and centrifugation to obtain the desired mitochondrial fractions. The study also involved maintaining pH levels during the extraction process and measuring oxygen consumption using Krebs cycle intermediates. Protein determination and the use of antioxidants ensured consistent preparation of active mitochondria. The researchers developed a widely adaptable procedure for isolating mitochondria from difficult-to-handle plant tissues.

Missing Entities: 
1. Isolation technique involves tissue maceration, antioxidants, tannin-complexing agents, high buffer concentrations, modified Folin technique, and Janus green B test.
2. Key steps include liquid nitrogen treatment, pulverization, extraction with sucrose-containing solution, pH maintenance, centrifugation, dialysis, and addition of buffers and ATP.
3. Measurement of oxygen consumption with Krebs cycle intermediates and protein determination by Folin technique are used to assess respiratory activity and mitochondrial functions.
4. The isolated mitochondria exhibit specific appearance and characteristics.

tein of the particulate fractions prepared by our technique over a period 
of 2 weeks from bananas at two stages of ripeness are given in Table 3 
The consistent results obtained with these parameters may possibly indi- 
cate that this is a satisfactory way to prepare and store large quantities 
of tissue for later examination of mitochondrial properties while at the 
same time arresting changes in the tissue The reason for the lower re- 
spiratory rates with these preparations compared with those in Tables 1 
and 2 is that the mitochondria in Table 3 were prepared in the absence of 
ATP, MgCl,, and BSA which always causes a reduction in activity 
Mitochondria prepared by this technique from the pulp of both green 
and ripe banana fruit exhibited respiratory responses in the presence of 
ADP over that in its absence of up to 3-fold greater, had P/O values of 
up to 1.7 with succinate as substrate, and were shown to actively 
accumulate Ca in the presence of succinate and inorganic phosphate 
The method of mitochondrial preparation described here offers the 
advantages of consistent results and higher activity than reported by 
Tager (4) The capacity to carry out oxidative phosphorylation and ion 
accumulation, as we11 as the response to ADP, indicates that the in- 
creased activity is not due to excessive damage to the mitochondria during 
preparation The efficiency of the method may result from factors such 
as careful control of pH during extraction, preferential rupture of the 
nontannin-bearing cells, rapid combination of inert protein with pheno- 
lies, and inhibition of oxidation of phenslics to products inhibitory to 304 HAARD AND HULTIN 
mitochondrial function The fact that tissue as difficult to work with as 
green banana pulp can be handled easily indicates that the method may 
have wide applicability

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from the article are as follows:

1. "Attempts to prepare active mitochondria by variation of the method of tissue rupture, incorporation of agents such as cysteine, ascorbic acid, caffeine, polyvinylpyrrolidone, casein, gelatin, egg albumin, bovine serum albumin..."
2. "One problem that arises during the handling of plant tissue is maintenance of the tissue in one state for a prolonged period."
3. "Freezing tissue is often the method of choice but here problems might arise during either the freezing or thawing process."
4. "We were interested in determining the effect of time of storage on certain properties of mitochondria extracted from tissue frozen in liquid nitrogen and pulverized."
5. "The powder was stored in polyethylene bags at -30°C."
6. "The succinic oxidase activities and the protein of the particulate fractions prepared by our technique over a period of 2 weeks from bananas at two stages of ripeness are given in Table 3."
7. "Mitochondria prepared by this technique from the pulp of both green and ripe banana fruit exhibited respiratory responses in the presence of ADP over that in its absence of up to 3-fold greater."
8. "Had P/O values of up to 1.7 with succinate as substrate."
9. "Were shown to actively accumulate Ca in the presence of succinate and inorganic phosphate."
10. "The method of mitochondrial preparation described here offers the advantages of consistent results and higher activity than reported by Tager (4)."
11. "The capacity to carry out oxidative phosphorylation and ion accumulation, as well as the response to ADP, indicates that the increased activity is not due to excessive damage to the mitochondria during preparation."
12. "The efficiency of the method may result from factors such as careful control of pH during extraction, preferential rupture of the nontannin-bearing cells, rapid combination of inert protein with phenolics, and inhibition of oxidation of phenolics to products inhibitory to mitochondrial function."

**Conclusion:**
The article mentions several steps for mitochondrial isolation, including the variation of tissue rupture method, freezing tissue, storage of tissue powder at -30°C, and the use of specific agents. However, the article does not provide a complete and detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation. Some steps, such as the specific agents used and the details of the extraction process, are not described. The article does provide information on the properties and activities of the isolated mitochondria, indicating the effectiveness of the method.

summary: Revised Summary: Efficient preparation of active mitochondria involves variations in tissue rupture methods, such as liquid nitrogen freezing and pulverization. Key compounds like cysteine, ascorbic acid, and caffeine, as well as proteins like polyvinylpyrrolidone, casein, gelatin, and egg albumin, are used to enhance the process. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is also present in the tissue. The method ensures the maintenance of mitochondrial properties during storage at -30°C. Mitochondria prepared using this technique exhibit high respiratory rates and increased activity. They also carry out oxidative phosphorylation and ion accumulation in the presence of ADP, ATP, MgCl2, and inorganic phosphate. The approach offers consistent results, higher activity, and wider applicability beyond bananas. The use of liquid nitrogen and the thawing process affect the stability of succinic oxidase activity. Mitochondrial homogenate is used to study mitochondrial function. Ion accumulation and phenolics play a role in mitochondrial properties. The liquid nitrogen method yields higher succinic oxidase activities compared to other methods. Proper storage of the tissue is crucial for maintaining mitochondrial properties. Mitochondria prepared from green and ripe banana pulp show significant respiratory responses and the ability to accumulate calcium and inorganic phosphate. The method described offers consistent results, higher activity, and wider applicability for handling difficult tissues. Factors such as pH control contribute to the efficiency of the method. Understanding mitochondrial properties and function is essential for studying cellular metabolism.

article:
Chunk # 2.
We have prepared active mitochondria with this 
method from the peel of banana fruits, the pulp of preclimacteric Early 
Macintosh apples, and the pulp of White Rose potatoes The Qo, (N) 
values for these tissues were 520, 445, and 561, respectively 
SUMMARY 
A method is described for the preparation of mitochondria of high 
and consistent respiratory activity from plant tissue It utilizes freezing 
the tissue in liquid nitrogen and maceration while in the frozen state 
Inert proteins and ATP and MgCl, are added during isolation The 
procedure gives comparable results with mitochondria from the pulp of 
green bananas and banana peel as compared to the pulp of ripe fruit 
The method may have general applicability to the preparation of mito- 
chondria from many plant tissues which are ordinarily difficult to handle 
because of high concentrations of acids and protein-complexing 
substances 
REFERENCES 
1 CA-YLIN, P B., Proc Am Sot Hod Sci 83, 217 (1963) 
2 VERLXXJR, J D., Plant Physiol 40, 1003 (1965) 
3 JONES, J D., AND HULME, A C., Biochem J 88, 44 (1963) 
4 TAGER, J M., X African J Sci 55, 324 (1958) 
5 LOWRY, 0 H., ROSEBIUKJGH, N J., FARR, A L., AND RANDALL, R J., J Biol Chem 
193, 265 (1951) 
6 ALLFREY, V., The isolation of subcellular components, in “The Cell,” (J Brachet 
and A E Mirsky, eds.), Vol I, p 266 Academic Press, New York, 1959

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "A method is described for the preparation of mitochondria of high and consistent respiratory activity from plant tissue."
2. "It utilizes freezing the tissue in liquid nitrogen and maceration while in the frozen state."
3. "Inert proteins and ATP and MgCl2 are added during isolation."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide specific details about the freezing and maceration process.
- The concentrations or amounts of the inert proteins, ATP, and MgCl2 are not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**

The article describes a method for isolating mitochondria from plant tissue using freezing and maceration. However, important details about the freezing and maceration process, as well as the concentrations or amounts of the added components, are missing. The protocol's general applicability to other plant tissues is mentioned, but no additional information is provided.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondria, the subcellular components responsible for ATP production and energy metabolism, can be isolated from various plant tissues, including the pulp of green bananas, banana peel, and ripe fruit. The preparation method involves freezing the tissue with liquid nitrogen and maceration while in the frozen state. Inert proteins, ATP, and MgCl are added during isolation to ensure stability and functionality. The study also investigated the isolation of mitochondria from the peel of banana fruits, the pulp of Early Macintosh apples, and the pulp of White Rose potatoes. The frozen state of mitochondria is crucial for maintaining their integrity and functionality. Respiratory activity, a measure of their functionality and metabolic processes, was comparable to mitochondria from ripe fruit. Handling plant tissues is challenging due to high concentrations of acids and protein-complexing substances. The Cell, authored by J. Brachet and A.E. Mirsky, provides valuable information on this process. (CA-YLIN, 1963; VERLXXJR, 1965; JONES & HULME, 1963; TAGER, 1958; LOWRY et al., 1951; ALLFREY, 1959)

topics.topic: Freezing and maceration method for isolation
topics.justification: The summary mentions that the preparation method involves freezing the tissue with liquid nitrogen and maceration while in the frozen state.

topics.topic: Importance of maintaining the frozen state of mitochondria
topics.justification: The summary highlights the crucial role of maintaining the frozen state of mitochondria for their integrity and functionality.

topics.topic: Respiratory activity as a measure of functionality
topics.justification: The summary mentions that respiratory activity is used as a measure of the functionality and metabolic processes of mitochondria.

topics.topic: Challenges in handling plant tissues for isolation
topics.justification: The summary discusses the challenges in handling plant tissues due to high concentrations of acids and protein-complexing substances.

topics.topic: Reference to the study in The Cell by J. Brachet and A.E. Mirsky
topics.justification: The summary mentions that The Cell, authored by J. Brachet and A.E. Mirsky, provides valuable information on the process of isolating mitochondria from plant tissues.

hypothetical_questions.question: How are mitochondria isolated from various plant tissues, such as green banana pulp, banana peel, and ripe fruit?
What are the key components and steps involved in the preparation method for isolating mitochondria from plant tissues?
Imagine you are a scientist studying mitochondria isolation. How would you modify the freezing and maceration process to optimize the yield and functionality of mitochondria?
How does the respiratory activity of mitochondria isolated from the peel of banana fruits and the pulp of Early Macintosh apples compare to those isolated from ripe fruit?
Have you ever encountered challenges in handling plant tissues for scientific experiments? How did you overcome them?
